caption: Cross-framework reproduction asks whether the intervention principle, rather than framework-specific behavior, explains PhyCustom-style gains on FLUX.
raw_prompt: A professional academic figure for a NeurIPS / ICML paper. Create a clean conceptual illustration with three panels arranged left-to-right on a white background. Left panel: a reference concept and a text prompt entering an original customization method, labeled "Original PhyCustom setting," with blue and gray arrows. Middle panel: a large bridge labeled "Cross-framework reproduction" connecting to a FLUX backbone icon, showing possible confounders floating around the bridge such as "autodiff," "numerics," "adapter placement," and "optimization dynamics" in small gray callout bubbles. Right panel: five lightweight adaptation variants entering the FLUX model, with one highlighted in orange and labeled "attention-only + prompt-swapped decoupling." Below the right panel, show four evaluation criteria as compact gauges: descriptor consistency, background leakage, reference fidelity, and realism. Use blue boxes for standard components, orange for the highlighted best variant, gray arrows for flow, and concise labels suitable for top-tier conference presentation.
full_prompt:
Create a professional academic figure for the 'Introduction' section of a research paper about: Reproduce PhyCustom on Flux.

Figure description: A professional academic figure for a NeurIPS / ICML paper. Create a clean conceptual illustration with three panels arranged left-to-right on a white background. Left panel: a reference concept and a text prompt entering an original customization method, labeled "Original PhyCustom setting," with blue and gray arrows. Middle panel: a large bridge labeled "Cross-framework reproduction" connecting to a FLUX backbone icon, showing possible confounders floating around the bridge such as "autodiff," "numerics," "adapter placement," and "optimization dynamics" in small gray callout bubbles. Right panel: five lightweight adaptation variants entering the FLUX model, with one highlighted in orange and labeled "attention-only + prompt-swapped decoupling." Below the right panel, show four evaluation criteria as compact gauges: descriptor consistency, background leakage, reference fidelity, and realism. Style guidelines:
Simple, clean diagram illustrating a key concept or intuition. Include before/after or problem/solution comparison. Keep it understandable at a glance.

 The image should be in a clean, professional ACADEMIC style suitable for a top-tier AI/ML research paper (NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR). Use a white or light background. Use clear labels and annotations. Avoid excessive decoration. Use a consistent color palette. Text should be legible at column width (~3.25 inches). Style: technical illustration, vector-like, clean lines.

caption: FLIP reproduces PhyCustom on FLUX by combining frozen-backbone generation, lightweight adapters, and intervention-aware comparison paths.
raw_prompt: A professional academic figure for a NeurIPS / ICML / ICLR paper. Show a clean left-to-right architecture diagram on a white background. On the left, two inputs enter the system: a reference exemplar set labeled "reference images r" and a text prompt labeled "prompt p". These feed into a central large blue box labeled "Frozen FLUX backbone". Inside the backbone, highlight several adapter insertion points in orange, with callouts for "attention-only LoRA", "attention+MLP LoRA", and "selective block unfreezing". From the backbone, branch into two output paths: the original prompt path and a prompt-swapped path. These feed into comparison modules labeled "descriptor consistency", "background leakage", "reference fidelity", and "text alignment", all shown as compact gray metric heads. A final loss aggregation box combines these into "FLIP objective". Use blue boxes for backbone components, orange highlights for trainable adaptation modules, gray arrows for data flow, and concise labels suitable for a top-tier ML conference figure.

caption: Prompt-swapped output-space decoupling creates counterfactual comparisons that expose descriptor entanglement and background leakage.
raw_prompt: A professional academic figure for a NeurIPS / ICML / ICLR paper. Create a top-to-bottom method flowchart with two parallel branches. At the top, show a shared reference exemplar set feeding into two prompts: "original prompt p_a" and "swapped prompt p_b". Both enter the same frozen FLUX model with orange adapter modules. In the middle, show two generated images or output feature boxes labeled "x_a" and "x_b". Below, add comparison arrows between the outputs with three highlighted comparison heads: "descriptor confusion", "background leakage", and "reference fidelity". A final orange box at the bottom is labeled "decoupling loss" and sends a gradient arrow back to the adapter modules only. Use blue boxes for model paths, orange boxes for trainable or loss-related components, gray comparison arrows, white background, and clear legible labels for conference-paper readability.
